text: The pharmacology curriculum organized analgesics into several major classes: opioids (full mu, partial mu, kappa), NSAIDs, alpha-2 adrenoceptor agonists, NMDA antagonists, gabapentinoids, adjuncts including tramadol and antidepressants, and newer targeted agents such as anti-NGF monoclonal antibodies. For each class, tell me: the primary receptor or enzyme it targets, one clinically important species-specific consideration that changes how you use it, and one major adverse effect you actively monitor for.

followups.text: Based on the content covered in this fellowship, you described tramadol. Specifically: what is the name of the active metabolite responsible for its mu-opioid analgesic effect, which hepatic enzyme converts tramadol to that metabolite, why do many dogs have a limited analgesic response via this pathway, and in which species does tramadol have well-supported analgesic efficacy at what specific dose?

Based on the content covered in this fellowship, describe the cardiovascular sequence that occurs after dexmedetomidine administration — the initial hemodynamic change, the reflex response, and the net cardiovascular state at 15 minutes — and name the patient population in which this sequence is most dangerous.

text: An 11-year-old domestic shorthair cat presents with IRIS Stage 3 CKD, bilateral elbow osteoarthritis confirmed radiographically, and an acute-on-chronic neuropathic pain flare following a dog-bite wound to the hindlimb. Construct a multimodal pharmacologic analgesic plan. For each drug you include, justify why it is appropriate given this patient's comorbidities. Explicitly address why you have included or excluded: long-term NSAIDs, an anti-NGF monoclonal antibody such as Solensia (frunevetmab), gabapentin, and a home opioid option.

followups.text: Based on the content covered in this fellowship, you included gabapentin. In an IRIS Stage 3 to 4 CKD cat, how does the half-life of gabapentin change from its normal value, what does that mean for dosing interval or dose, and what clinical signs would alert you that the dose is too high for this patient?

Based on the content covered in this fellowship, explain specifically why buprenorphine is the preferred home opioid for this cat rather than a full mu agonist — include both the pharmacologic rationale and the specific route of administration that is practical and well-tolerated for owner use at home in cats.

text: This fellowship covered several high-risk prescribing scenarios: NSAIDs combined with corticosteroids; opioid diversion and controlled substance compliance; alpha-2 agonists in cardiovascularly compromised patients; bisphosphonate use in patients with concurrent dental disease; and opioid stacking. Choose two of these and for each: describe the specific mechanism of harm, identify the patient-level red flags that should trigger a prescribing pause, and describe the practice-level safeguard you would put in place.

followups.text: Based on the content covered in this fellowship, a client phones to report their dog is on carprofen prescribed by you, and a second veterinarian has just prescribed prednisone for an allergic reaction. Walk me through exactly what you say to that client and to the prescribing veterinarian — what the specific mechanism of harm is, what needs to happen, and over what timeframe.

Based on the content covered in this fellowship, you are considering zoledronate for a dog with osteosarcoma pain. Walk me through the administration protocol exactly as taught: the dose range, the required diluent, the exact infusion duration, the maximum total dose, and the pre-existing condition that must be screened for and is an absolute contraindication to treatment.

text: Opioid-sparing and opioid-free multimodal analgesia was a recurring theme throughout this fellowship. In your current practice environment, what will it realistically take to move a meaningful percentage of your surgical and chronic pain patients toward opioid-sparing protocols? Where do you still view opioids as non-negotiable, and for those cases, how has this fellowship changed how you use them?

followups.text: Based on the content covered in this fellowship, describe a specific opioid-sparing perioperative analgesic protocol — with actual drug names, doses, and routes — for a 5-year-old dog undergoing elective TPLO. Include at least one NMDA antagonist component with its dose and route, and at least one locoregional technique appropriate for this surgery.

Based on the content covered in this fellowship, you identified cases where full mu opioids remain necessary. For two of those scenarios, describe the lowest-risk opioid choice and delivery method this fellowship would support, and explain how the choice differs between cats and dogs in terms of the route used and the monitoring required.
